title: Check if Two Trees are Identical

statement.markdown: Given the roots of two binary trees `root1` and `root2`, write a function to check if they are identical or not. Two binary trees are considered identical if they are structurally identical and the nodes have the same value. A binary tree node is defined as a tree node with the following attributes: `val`, `left`, and `right`.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer N1, the number of nodes in the first tree. The next N1 lines contain the node values in a pre-order traversal of the first tree.
The next line contains a single integer N2, the number of nodes in the second tree. The next N2 lines contain the node values in a pre-order traversal of the second tree.
statement.outputFormat: A single line containing 'True' if the two trees are identical and 'False' otherwise.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in each tree.
The values of the nodes in the trees.

examples.explanation: Both trees have 3 nodes with preorder values [1, 2, 3].
The second tree has a different node value.

hints.text: To check if two trees are identical, we need to compare their structures and node values. We can start by checking if both trees are empty, in which case they are identical.
If one tree is empty and the other is not, they are not identical. We also need to compare the values of the root nodes. If they are different, the trees are not identical.

To compare the structures and node values of the two trees, we can use a recursive approach. We can define a helper function that takes two nodes as input and checks if they are identical. This function can recursively call itself for the left and right child nodes.

editorial.title: Checking Identical Trees: A Journey from Brute Force to Optimality
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that two trees are identical if and only if their structures are the same and their corresponding nodes have the same values. This suggests a recursive or iterative approach that checks both the structure and the node values simultaneously.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve generating all possible tree structures for each given tree and then comparing these structures and their node values. This approach is correct but results in an explosion of possibilities, making it extremely slow, with a time complexity much worse than the known O(N1 + N2).
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient approach is to use recursion or iteration to traverse both trees simultaneously, comparing node values and structures at each step.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves a recursive function that checks if two trees are identical by comparing their roots and then recursively checking their left and right subtrees. This approach is known to have a time complexity of O(N1 + N2) and a space complexity of O(h1 + h2), where h1 and h2 are the heights of the two trees.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for the optimal solution hinges on the fact that two trees are identical if and only if their roots are equal and their left and right subtrees are identical. This recursive definition matches the recursive structure of the solution, ensuring correctness.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run with two simple identical trees (e.g., both being a single node with the same value) and two simple non-identical trees helps verify the solution's correctness.
commonMistakes: Not checking for the base case where both trees are empty.
Not handling the case where one tree is empty and the other is not.
Incorrectly comparing node values or tree structures.
Not properly handling recursive function calls, leading to stack overflow for very tall trees.
Ignoring the possibility of different tree structures with the same number of nodes.

functionSignature.java: public boolean isSameTree(TreeNode root1, TreeNode root2);
functionSignature.python: def isSameTree(self, root1: TreeNode, root2: TreeNode) -> bool:
